reasoning: The FRED statistical model estimates a 0.49 probability of exceeding the recent high of 6.97 based on a mix of linear mean-reversion (0.31) and seasonal uptrends (0.67). We observed a sharp jump in the yield in the final days of February (from 6.76 on Feb 26 to 6.97 on Feb 28). This was driven by a combination of month-end accrued interest adjustments outlined in the ICE BofA methodology, and a real market 'risk-off' shift on Feb 27 (S&P 500 down ~0.8% following hot inflation data, pushing Treasury yields down and credit spreads wider). Because 6.97 is a localized spike above the February ~6.75 average, short-term mean reversion suggests the yield is likelier to be lower by March 8. For March 31, uncertainty naturally increases toward 0.50, and macroeconomic headwinds such as delayed rate-cut expectations and sticky inflation provide more fundamental runway for yields to climb back up. Therefore, the probability scales up toward the 0.5 mark for the longer horizon.
